title: • Comics don’t force escalation the way film does—instead, they c
content:
• Comics don’t force escalation the way film does—instead, they can let the reader linger or rush through moments.
• Your job is to design pages that naturally guide pacing.

Activity: Translating Conflict into Comics

💡 Take a scene from Whiplash and rewrite it as a comic using both McKee and McCloud’s principles.

Step 1: Choose a Scene from Whiplash

Example: The “Not My Tempo” scene.

Step 2: Apply McKee’s Story Structure
• Conflict: Andrew is eager to impress Fletcher, but Fletcher humiliates him.
• Decision: Andrew pushes himself harder instead of giving up.
• Escalation: Fletcher’s abuse gets worse as Andrew tries harder.

Step 3: Apply McCloud’s Visual Principles
• Panel Layout: Use a tight grid to create a sense of control (like in Watchmen).
• Closure: Show Fletcher’s raised hand in one panel, and Andrew flinching in the next—but don’t show the actual hit.
context: From document: 1.1.3. | Narrative Transcends Medium | Subheading — chunk 6 (chunk 6/7)
